column1: **Prepared by:** [Your Name], CPA – Tax Preparation Services

Below is a concise “rule‑book” that applies to every line you will be reporting on the 2023 (or 2022, if you are filing for that year) federal Form 1040 and a typical state return.  If you live in a state with its own quirks (e.g., California, New York, Illinois, etc.), the concepts are the same; only the rates/credits differ.

column4: Typical State Rule (most states)

column3:
• Must be reported on **Schedule C** (Profit or Loss from Business).
• Net profit (gross receipts – allowable expenses) flows to **Form 1040, line 3** (Other Income).
• You also owe **self‑employment (SE) tax** on the net profit (Schedule SE). The SE tax rate is 15.3 % (12.4 % Social Security + 2.9 % Medicare) on **92.35 %** of net profit.
• Half of the SE tax is an “above‑the‑line” deduction on **Form 1040, line 10** (Adjusted Gross Income).
column4:
• Most states start with the same federal AGI (or a slightly modified version).
• Some states **do not allow the half‑SE‑tax deduction**; they tax the full net profit.
• A few states have a separate “self‑employment tax” (e.g., California’s LLC fee).

column3:
• Deductible on **Schedule A** (Itemized Deductions) if the loan is a qualified residence loan.
• The amount reported on Form 1098 is the limit; you cannot exceed $750,000 of acquisition debt (or $1 million for older loans).
• If you **take the standard deduction**, you **cannot** also claim mortgage interest.
column4:
• Most states mirror the federal treatment (interest is allowed if you itemize).
• Some states **add‑back** a portion of mortgage interest (e.g., New York).

column3:
Two categories: **Qualifying Child** or **Qualifying Relative**.
• *Qualifying Child* – must be under **age 24** and a **full‑time student**, must have lived with you > 6 months, and **must not have provided more than half of his/her own support**.
• *Qualifying Relative* – any age, must have **gross income ≤ $4,700** (2023) and you must provide **more than half of support**.
• The dependent must have a valid SSN (or ITIN) and you must be able to claim the **Child Tax Credit** (if under 17) or the **Credit for Other Dependents** ($500) if older.
• The personal exemption was eliminated by the TCJA, so there is no “exemption amount” to subtract.
column4:
• Most states use the same federal definition.
• A few states (e.g., Illinois) have **additional dependent credits** that follow the federal definition.

column3:
For **2023**:
• **Single** – $13,850
• **Married Filing Jointly** – $27,700
• **Head of Household** – $20,800
These amounts are **indexed for inflation** each year.
If you **itemize** (Schedule A) you must compare the total of your itemized deductions to the standard amount and use the larger of the two.
column4:
• Most states have their own standard deduction (often a percentage of the federal amount).
• Some states **do not allow itemized deductions** at all (e.g., Colorado).
• The state may have a “personal exemption” that still exists even though the federal one does not.

Support test: if you paid > $9,000 toward his/her living expenses (rent, food, tuition, etc.) you can claim the dependent; otherwise you cannot.
column6: You **cannot automatically claim** the dependent.  You need to calculate the support you provided.  If you **did not** provide > ½ support, you must **remove the dependent** from the return.

column4: “State tax form shows a computed tax liability that does not match the AGI shown.”
column5: Most states start with the **same AGI** you report on the federal return, **unless** they add back items (e.g., the half‑SE‑tax deduction).  If you omitted the half‑SE‑tax deduction on the federal return, the state AGI will be **higher** than the federal AGI, causing the mismatch you observed.
column6: You need to **re‑calculate AGI** correctly on the federal return first; the state AGI will then line up (or you will know exactly what the state adds back).

column3: *You need to supply the amount shown on your W‑2 box 2.*  Subtract that from $8,782.27 to get the balance due or refund.

Start with $72,403.5 (AGI).  If the state **does not allow** the half‑SE‑tax deduction, add back $296.5 → $72,700.  Then subtract the state’s standard deduction (often ~$5,000‑$6,000 for single filers).  The exact tax will depend on your state’s rates.

column3: If you paid tuition for the student, you may be eligible for the **American Opportunity Credit** (up to $2,500) or the **Lifetime Learning Credit** (up to $2,000).
column4: Provide Form 8863 with the tuition statements (Form 1098‑T).  This is a *tax credit*, not a deduction, and it can be claimed even if the student is not a dependent **provided you meet the support test**.
